data.title: August Jobs Report: Another Weak Report Heightens Pressure For Rate Cuts
data.description:
The August jobs report ends a busy week of data showing just how fragile the labor market is. Hiring momentum has slowed, gains continue to be concentrated in just a handful of industries, and public sector employment is beginning to meaningfully weigh on the headline numbers. The household data points to a gradual loosening in the labor supply, with specific age groups facing more pain than others. Together, these dynamics highlight a labor market that is no longer uniformly strong but softening in key areas that will spur the Federal Reserve into action.

Establishment Data

US nonfarm payroll employment came in weak again in August, increasing by just 22,000, which is a deceleration from the 79,000 jobs added in July and well below the consensus estimate of 75,000. This number looks more like the soft prints in May and June than the bounce back in July, suggesting that there is a real slowdown in hiring that can’t be ignored. With this print, the three-month average of job gains remains below 30,000 for the second straight month. The details of the report tell a similar story of the headline employment number being carried by a few industries.

The first thing to note in the details is that there was a divergence between private and public sector hiring in August. Private payrolls increased 38,000, still below expectations of around 75,000 but notably stronger than the headline number. This means that government payrolls declined by -16,000, the second largest month behind May 2025 in the last three years. The shedding of jobs in the public sector was driven by the federal government as the Trump administration’s efforts to downsize the federal workforce continue to impact the labor market. So far this year, government payrolls (federal, state, and local) have only increased by 8,000. In the same period last year, government payrolls increased by 323,000.

Within the private sector, hiring conditions weren’t particularly strong. The goods sector saw a decline in employment for the fourth straight month at -25,000, which is also the worst decline since October 2024 and the second worst since March 2023. All three industries tracked in the goods sector lost jobs, including the manufacturing sector (-12,000), which is also on a four-month streak of job losses. Offsetting the decline in goods sector employment was a 63,000 increase in the services sector, which looks like a solid increase, but the sector’s gains were driven primarily by two industries: education & health services (+46,000) and leisure & hospitality (+28,000). The other services industries saw job losses or no growth, including professional services (-17,000) and information (-5,000). The conclusion is one that we have seen before: payroll employment gains are being propped up by two industries, education & health services and leisure & hospitality.

Household Data

Alongside a weakening in hiring came an increase in the unemployment rate in the Household data. The unemployment rate ticked up 0.1 ppts to 4.3% in August, in line with the consensus forecast. The more precise increase in the rate was about 7.5 bps, or an increase from 4.25% to 4.32%. In terms of raw numbers, there was a 148,000 increase in the unemployed population and an even larger 288,000 increase in the employed population. And overall, there was a strong 436,000 increase in the labor force, which caused the labor force participation rate to tick up 0.1 ppt to 62.3%, reversing a trend of a participation rate that has been falling since April. These general statistics all suggest that the supply side of the labor market is loosening but not at an alarming rate.

While the overall unemployment rate only saw a gradual increase, there were some interesting dynamics for certain age groups.

  • The first thing to note was a -1.3 ppts drop in the unemployment rate for the 16 to 19-year-old segment after spiking 1.8 ppts over the summer. While this could be tracked to changes related to the school year, this data is seasonally adjusted, so volatility like this shouldn’t happen. I hypothesize that summer employment opportunities must have been thinner than usual for that group, which resulted in the exaggerated moves.

  • Moving up to the next age segment, we see an even more concerning trend. The unemployment rate for 20- to 24-year-olds, correlating to individuals getting out of high school or college to join the workforce, jumped 1.3 ppts to 9.2%, the highest since May 2021. A surge in joblessness in this segment typically only happens when there is a recession coming or currently happening. However, there is a chance that this instance could be an exception since AI is increasingly encroaching on young professionals’ abilities to find an entry-level job.
  • Finally, there are the prime age (25 to 54 years) and 55+ age segments, which both stand out as stronger than the two younger segments. In August, the prime-age unemployment rate remained unchanged at 3.6%, even though there was an increase of about 50,000 in the raw values. The monthly trend was similar for the 55+ segment, where the unemployment rate remained at 2.9%. Most significantly, both of these segments have seen no increase in their unemployment rates in the last year, suggesting that the labor market for more skilled and established workers is actually quite solid.

These details matter because they show how broad labor market trends are playing out across different groups. In effect, there are two labor markets unfolding: one where younger workers are struggling to find jobs and falling behind in starting their careers and financial lives, and another where prime-age and older workers feel relatively secure and are not facing the same risks of layoffs. As noted earlier, weakness in younger cohorts has historically been a reliable signal of recession risk, often more telling than a small uptick, such as a 0.1 ppt increase, in the overall unemployment rate.

Wage Growth

The wage growth data from the Establishment data was mostly consistent with the cooling in hiring seen in the headline job gains numbers. Average hourly earnings (AHE) increased 0.3% MoM in August, and the annual increase slowed from 3.9% YoY in July to 3.7% YoY, matching June for the lowest rate since July 2024. The cooling in wage growth came from the services sector, where AHE increased 0.3% MoM and was up 3.6% YoY (down from 3.8% YoY). This was partially offset by a 0.4% MoM increase in goods sector AHE growth, which caused the annual rate to tick back up to 4.0% YoY. Overall, wage growth does continue to be a bit sticky, as it hasn’t returned to the post-pandemic lows of last summer, but since it is in a gradual decline, it isn’t a focus for the Fed, which is more concerned about layoffs picking up, leading to a higher unemployment rate.

Market Reaction

Markets reacted to the large disappointment in the headline job gains number as one might expect. The moves across asset classes reflected an increase in rate cut expectations, similar to moves seen earlier this week when weak numbers were also released in the JOLTS survey and the ADP employment report.

  • Equity futures reacted positively to the soft jobs report, rising in the hour or so after the release by about 0.4%. Futures trended around there until market open, when trading started to move lower. Two hours after the jobs report was released, the S&P 500 was down around -0.4%, and the NASDAQ gains were erased. Choppy trading can happen when increased rate cut expectations come up against a higher probability of a recession. Since the market has more or less judged a September rate cut as certain, that positive factor is likely being overwhelmed by recession fears in today’s trading.

  • The bond market and the US dollar are trading more one-dimensionally. The weakness in labor demand seen in this morning's report is leading bond traders to be more confident in a rate cut this month and in October and December. The large declines in the 1-year and 2-year rates, or around -11 to -12 bps, price in more rate cuts over the next six months. Recession fears are also hitting long-term yields as the 10-year yield drops -8 bps and the 30-year yield is down around -7 bps, reflecting lower growth expectations. The former is now moving towards 4.0% and is at its lowest since early April. The dollar is following yields, with the US dollar index (DXY) down -0.6% on the day, reflecting moves in EURUSD (+0.6%), GBPUSD (+0.6%), and USDJPY (-0.9%).

Today’s jobs report is the ultimate dovish finish to a week full of labor market updates. First, we had a larger-than-expected decline in job openings on Wednesday, next a disappointing print in the ADP employment report on Thursday, and now an even larger miss in the nonfarm payrolls number. Taken together, there is plenty of evidence to confirm the suspicions from the July jobs report revisions that the labor market cooling has continued, especially on the demand side in both the private and public sectors. Notably, the trend over the last few months is showing similar characteristics to the cooling that occurred in the summer of 2024, which resulted in a 50 bps rate cut.

Suddenly, there is a 73.4% chance that the Fed funds rate is at least 75 bps lower after the December FOMC meeting, according to CME Group’s FedWatch tool, which is up significantly from 45.8% just yesterday. The sharp turn in the probability doesn’t just reflect the softness in the labor market data this week, but it also incorporates the likelihood that the Trump administration successfully penetrates the Federal Reserve’s independence. In any case, investors should be prepared for the Fed to restart its cutting cycle in September and anticipate the policy rate dropping in the following 12 months.

data.title: MEDI Group Readies $15 Million U.S. IPO Plan
data.description:
MEDI’s Revenue Is Falling, And The IPO Is Pricey

MEDI Group Limited (MEDG) has filed to raise $15 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

MEDG provides a range of beauty and aesthetic personal services in the greater Hong Kong region.

However, the firm’s topline revenue is falling from an already small base and its valuation expectations appear excessive.

My outlook on the MEDG IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does MEDI Do?

Hong Kong, China-based MEDI Group Limited was founded to offer medical cosmetology and traditional beauty treatments via its locations in Hong Kong, PRC.

Management is led by founder, Chairman and CEO Mr. Ng Hon Kin, who has been with the firm since its inception in March 2021 and has more than twenty years of experience in the medical aesthetic industry, according to the firm.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Medical aesthetic services

  • Normal beauty services

The pie charts below show the revenue contribution from the firm's segments and locations:

MEDI has booked fair market value investment of $2.3 million as of December 31, 2024 from investors, including Many Trillion International, Express Essential Holdings, Winlover, Ace Miracle Group and Able Talent Business Limited.

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have dropped, as the figures below indicate:

Selling & Marketing

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Six Mos. Ended Dec. 31, 2024

35.6%

FYE June 30, 2024

48.4%

FYE June 30, 2023

44.8%

(Source - SEC)

The Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of Selling and Marketing expense, improved slightly to negative (0.5x), a poor result, in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

Selling & Marketing

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Six Mos. Ended Dec. 31, 2024

-0.5

FYE June 30, 2024

-0.6

(Source - SEC)

What Is MEDI’s Market?

According to a 2024 market research report by Grand View Research, the market for medical spa treatments in China was an estimated $607 million in 2023 and is expected to reach $1.8 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 16.8% from 2024 to 2030, a high rate of estimated growth.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are a strong expected growth rate from the Body Shaping & Contouring service segment, while facial treatments will still have a significant market share.

Also, the chart below shows the expected growth trajectory of the market through 2030:

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

There are over 300 medical aesthetic centers in Hong Kong and thousands of traditional beauty salons, according to a report by Frost & Sullivan (PDF) so the company faces extreme competition and relatively low barriers to entry.

The firm pursues locations near to where its clients work or live and targets consumers in the middle market.

As of June 30, 2024 the company counted 2,600 customers with unexpired prepaid services packages, 'most of whom are repeat customers who made their first purchases with us more than three years ago.'

MEDI’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Dropping topline revenue

  • Variable gross profit and gross margin

  • Fluctuating operating profit

  • Higher cash used in operations

Below are major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

Total Revenue

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source - SEC)

As of December 31, 2024, MEDI had $3,875 in cash and $10.5 million in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, of negative ($1.1 million).

MEDI’s IPO Plan

MEDG intends to sell approximately 3.33 million shares of Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $15 million, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, as detailed in the most recent prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders will be entitled to twenty (20) votes per share and the company will be a ‘controlled company’ immediately post-IPO.

Shareholders are also offering via a different prospectus up to 4.22 million shares for resale.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the standard 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus.

Dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $4.30, which is within the typical range for a company at IPO.

The company’s enterprise value at IPO (assuming no underwriter options) will approximate $124 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 10.99%.

According to MEDI’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

approximately [5]% to set up our research and development capacity;

approximately [25]% to penetrate and further expand into new and existing geographical markets;

approximately [65]% for general working capital and other general corporate purposes; and

approximately [5]% as advisory fee payment.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, management said that none of its entities are subject to legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are American Trust Investment Services and WestPark Capital.

Here is a table of relevant capitalization and valuation figures for the firm:

Measure [TTM]

Amount

Market Capitalization at IPO

$136,499,999

Enterprise Value

$124,189,999

Price / Sales

24.79

EV / Revenue

22.56

EV / EBITDA

148.08

Earnings Per Share

$0.02

P/E Ratio

215.25

Operating Margin

11.89%

Comprehensive Margin

11.71%

Float To Outstanding Shares Ratio

10.99%

SBC as Percentage of Free Cash Flow

0%

Dilution to New Shareholders

$4.30

Revenue Growth Rate

-15.70%

(Glossary Of Terms)

(Source - SEC)

MEDI’s Revenue Is Falling From A Low Base

MEDI Group is seeking US public capital market investment to fund its working capital and expansion requirements.

MEDG’s financials have produced falling topline revenue, fluctuating gross profit and gross margin, variable operating profit and increased cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024, was negative ($1.1 million).

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenue has dropped, and its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple was negative (0.5x) in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay dividends as and when the board of directors determines to do so. It is under a variety of restrictions, including under Cayman Islands law and potential restrictions on the movement of capital from its operating subsidiaries in China.

MEDG’s recent capital spending history shows it has continued to spend on capital expenditures even as it has generated negative operating cash flow.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its footprint within Hong Kong and outside the region and broadening its offerings to cater to the fast-growing male customer segment.

The market opportunity for providing medical spa and beauty treatment services is moderate but expected to grow substantially in the coming years.

However, MEDI faces intense competition from hundreds to thousands of other locations offering similar services.

Additional risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘foreign private issuer’ and ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows management to produce less information for shareholders.

Also, the firm has geographic and customer concentration risks.

MEDI has significant related party transactions, especially in the area of personal guarantees of bank loans, as more fully detailed here.

As to valuation, MEDI is seeking an EV/Revenue multiple of 22.5x and EV/EBITDA multiple of 148.7x, despite falling revenue from a low base.

Given the numerous risks and excessive valuation expectations of management, my outlook on the MEDI Group IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Forget Nvidia: Broadcom Is The New Face Of AI
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) reported Q3 '25 Thursday after the bell, confirming our belief that it is the new face of AI, backed by a stronger push from tier 1 guys toward developing AI custom ASIC and tailwinds on its networking business, also tied to AI. The company has now secured $10B in orders for its custom AI chips, coined XPUs, from a fourth customer, bringing relief to investors that the AI growth story persists.

Broadcom is now sitting on about $30B-$38B worth of AI custom ASIC orders, according to HSBC estimates based on CoWoS consumption, and management is guiding for +60% Y/Y AI-related revenue growth next year. We remain bullish.

Mystery number four

We know that Broadcom expects this fourth mystery customer to “materially boost” AI revenue, adding on what’s already been better-than-expected growth this quarter, with AI-related revenue surging 18% sequentially and 63% from a year ago to $5.2B (ahead of Broadcom’s own guidance for $5.1B). For the current quarter, i.e., Q4, Broadcom expects AI-related revenue to reach $6.2B, which would be an almost 20% sequential jump. The AI moat showed up on its total sales guidance for next quarter, too; management is forecasting 9% Q/Q growth to $17.8B, outpacing the consensus of $17B.

No one knows who this fourth customer is, but the speculation is that it’s OpenAI, based on earlier reports that the two are working on developing custom AI chips, according to the Financial Times. We agree with rumors that OpenAI is the new customer, given that last year, OpenAI was working on developing its own in-house chip with Broadcom and TSMC (TSM), but dropped out due to the high cost of the foundry plans. The consensus is that Broadcom’s solutions are superior on a performance and energy efficiency to cost ratio, and all the big tech players are moving over.

This quarter's results, and the addition of a new customer, confirm our bullish outlook on Broadcom; we see the stock outperforming the semi peer group and S&P 500 (SP500) benchmark, in H2 '25 and 2026, as the total addressable market or TAM of AI custom ASIC and AI networking expands and Broadcom remains best positioned to capture share.

AI Race: Nvidia versus Broadcom

Nvidia (NVDA) is the industry's go-to for AI when it comes to GPUs, owning 94% of the global GPU sales as of Q2, according to Jon Peddie Research. Although ASIC is gaining ground, the AI GPU market is still growing strong, with units sold growing 27% sequentially in Q2. Equally important is the fact that Nvidia remains Wall Street’s AI sweetheart, although we’re seeing this narrative shift a bit with China headwinds.

So, where does this leave Broadcom? Well, Broadcom is benefiting from the hyper demand for Nvidia GPUs to build AI servers via its networking business. CEO Hock Tan reminded everyone this quarter that

“for the past 20 years, Broadcom has developed for Ethernet networking that is entirely applicable to the challenges of scale up, scale out, and scale across in generative AI.”

So there is some stable footing on the current GPU-centered AI story, but the more exciting part is where the industry is moving, or more accurately, segmenting into: AI custom ASIC.

Now, the AI custom ASIC market is one in which Broadcom is easily the leader, followed by Marvell (MRVL), which lacks Broadcom’s scale and R&D capabilities. Within the custom ASIC market, estimated to grow at a CAGR of 8.23% between 2025-30, Broadcom owns an estimated 70% to 80% share as of mid-2025, with Google (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and China's Bytedance (BDNCE), parent of TikTok. Adding OpenAI to that mix means that Broadcom wins most of the AI infrastructure dollars spent on ASIC over Marvell, which is concentrated with fewer customers.

AI GPUs still are the favored solution over ASIC, but that’s slowly changing as the AI market segmentizes. Think of it like this: within the world of AI, you have training and inference. Training will always require GPUs, so that’s Nvidia’s playground through and through. Now, things are a bit more complicated when it comes to inference; you have three levels, depending on the cognitive demand required:

  • Low-complexity - narrow, specific tasks using basic models.

  • Mid-complexity - a little more complex, requiring adaptation to new data.

  • High-complexity - intellectual tasks that require human-like reasoning and adaptation.

Currently, Nvidia’s AI GPUs are used for all three, but next year and in 2027, ASICs will take over the low to mid-level complexity inference workloads. ASIC offers a more attractive performance-to-cost ratio for the low to mid-level inference workloads, not to mention that the appeal of ASIC is that it’s customizable depending on workload. You have Google developing TPUs (tensor processing units) while Meta’s working on Santa Barbara, and Amazon’s focused on its Inferentia and Trainium. ASIC fails on the higher complexity workloads and on training, but it does offer a niche that reduces tier 1 dependency on Nvidia and tailors the AI chips. So, over time, the ASIC market is going to take a chunk of Nvidia’s market share, and the good news is that Broadcom is leading that share expansion.

A lot of Marvell bulls will ask why we leave Marvell out of our discussion of the future of ASIC, and the reason isn’t because Marvell isn’t competing; it is, but it’s the fact that Marvell is struggling. The company is too small to do what Broadcom does as well as Broadcom does it. For the past three quarters, Marvell has crashed post-earnings for the same reason: lackluster AI growth. This quarter was the cherry on top, with data center sales forecasted to be flat sequentially as electro-optical ICs grow double-digit, but lower custom ASIC shipments drag down the segment. Management’s commentary on Q2 was positive with data center revenue up 69% Y/Y and 3% Q/Q, fueled by “robust AI demand,” but markets are forward looking and that growth is coming to a halt. Here’s what Marvell had to say about next quarter (emphasis added):

…we expect growth to be nonlinear in the custom business, with the fourth quarter substantially stronger than the third. As a result, we expect overall data center revenue in the third quarter to be flat sequentially with electro-optics strength offset by lower custom revenue.

Q3 Financials

Broadcom’s business extends way beyond the ASIC and networking side, and that overall business is growing. Semi solution revenue was up 9% Q/Q and 26% Y/Y to $9.2B, while non-AI related semi solutions were flat sequentially at $4B. We think the flat sequential growth is part of the U-shaped recovery that’s happening and will turn positive in mid-2026. The software side of the business has been on a tear since the VMWare acquisition, up 17% sequentially this quarter. VMware Cloud Foundation v9.0 has launched, demonstrating momentum with a software gross margin of 93% and an operating margin of 77%. Margins are under pressure as ASIC becomes a bigger part of the total mix, (remember ASIC is a low margin business) but we think Broadcom’s other high-margin business will offset the ASIC pressure.

For the quarter, management reported a non-GAAP gross margin of 78.4%, relatively strong, even higher than Nvidia and definitely higher than Marvell, but contracted 100 basis points. Backlog is strong, hitting $110B this quarter. The networking side of the business is just as important as the ASIC side in terms of top-line growth; Tomahawk 6 and Jericho4 should enable scale across multi-DC clusters.

Bottom Line:

Broadcom is the new face of AI; the stock has outperformed the S&P 500 and the semiconductor benchmark YTD, as shown below. The stock is up 120% over the past year.

Valuation is stretched, but we don't think investors should mind at all, considering Broadcom has the growth to back it up. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 45.5x, roughly 95% higher than peers, and forward EV/Sales is 23.55x against the sector average of 3.19x. The PEG stands at 1.93x, only slightly above the sector median of 1.82x. So, yes, the stock trades at a premium relative to the sector but broadly in line with its own five-year averages.

Broadcom is up over 37% since our Strong Buy call post-Q2 earnings, as shown above. EMA200, EMA50, and even EMA21 are acting as strong support at current levels. So, we're expecting higher lows and higher highs from here. Having said that, RSI has shot up to 73 at market open, which makes the stock a less attractive buy at current levels. So, while we do believe this is the best AI pick in terms of risk-reward, we don’t recommend rushing in at current levels. We think investors should wait for the post-earning excitement to cool down before adding.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FONAR: Downgrading To Hold On Poor Profitability Trends
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote about FONAR Corporation (NASDAQ:FONR) in December of last year, when we reaffirmed our bullish stance on the US-based MRI company, stating that the company's assets were too attractive to pass up. Shares, however, have failed to push higher over the past nine months or so, which means they have trailed the S&P's return (approximately +6%), given FONAR's continued unwillingness to pay a dividend.

The premise of our bullish-related commentary up to now also delved into the company's strong cash position, positive profitability, and zero debt, strengthening FONAR's investment case from a valuation perspective. Although muted growth rates & low trading volumes continue to be inherent risks in this play, we believed that FONAR's growing book value (on the back of strategic MRI investments in Florida & New York) would eventually reward shareholders over time.

Bearish Intermediate Technicals

Nevertheless, when we go to the technical chart, we see that FONAR printed lower lows when it bottomed in April of this year. Although technical analysis may have its detractors (especially when applied to micro-cap stocks), market psychology still applies in these plays, irrespective of lower participation. Suffice it to say, the multi-year bottoming pattern, which we had assumed was playing itself out, is now in jeopardy due to the defining lower lows earlier this year. Furthermore, lower lows now mean the stock has more overhead technical resistance to surmount, all things remaining equal.

Non-Binding Take-Private Proposal

On July 9th of this year, the company issued a press release stating that the board of directors had received a non-binding proposal to essentially take the company private. It was stated that the proposed acquisition group (led by Timothy Damadian) held just over 5% of the outstanding stock and that the deal would entail outstanding capital stock being bought at a 10% premium above the average closing share price in the three months preceding the 1st of July. Shares jumped on the news (taking out $16 a share by the following week), but considering where the share price was coming from (long-term shareholders were most likely registering paper losses due to the above-mentioned pattern of lower lows), this 'deal' in itself wasn't that incentivising to say the least.

Then, news of a supplemental proposal letter was released on the 18th of July, improving the offer to $17.25 a share with the setting up of a special committee to review the same. Nevertheless, further down on the press release is what existing shareholders (or prospective investors looking to buy FONAR stock before a possible buyout takes place) should be focused on, in our opinion.

FONAR cautions its shareholders and others considering trading in FONAR’s securities that FONAR has only recently received the supplemental proposal letter and no decisions have been made with respect to FONAR’s response to the proposed transaction. The proposal constitutes only an indication of interest by the Proposed Acquisition Group and does not constitute a binding commitment. There can be no assurance that any agreement will be executed or that the transaction contemplated in the proposal or any other transaction will be approved or consummated. No agreement, arrangement, or understanding between FONAR and the Proposed Acquisition Group relating to any proposed transaction will be created unless definitive documentation is executed and delivered by the appropriate parties.

Q3 Earnings - 2025

Operating income of $3.7 million in the third quarter (Earnings announced on 5/15/2025) was on par with the same quarter of 12 months previous year despite net revenues growing by 6% ($27.2 million). This means that operating margin fell, all things remaining equal, as SG&A costs rose to $8 million in the quarter. Management talked up the record number of scans in Q3, highlighting how the addition of high-field MRI machines in certain locations can add more value from the standpoint of widening the physician base, lending itself to more scanning opportunities over time.

Be the above as it may, we would have expected better profitability trends from FONAR, and here is where value investors in particular need to be cautious. Although the company's equity continues to increase, FONAR's trailing ROE comes in at a disappointing 4.76%, well behind its 5-year average of 6.57%. Furthermore, we can calculate FONAR's cost of equity (minimum return investors expect) by using the following formula, where we use the 10-year US treasury yield as our risk-free rate and a third-party calculation for our equity risk premium (Damodaran).

Cost of equity = Risk-Free Rate + (Beta) x (Equity Risk Premium)

Cost of equity for FONR = 4.21% + (0.96)(4.33%) = 8.37%

Therefore, it is evident that FONAR continues to destroy shareholder value, which explains why the stock continues to trade at a steep discount with respect to its assets (Price-to-book multiple of 0.57). In fact, the one outlier on FONAR's balance sheet is its growing receivables balance of $81+ million. Shareholders need to be probing here whether this cash can be collected at a faster clip & what is its long-term quality in a probable prolonged inflationary environment in the US.

To the above point, if improvements are not made here promptly, the longer a sizable gap remains between FONAR's return on equity & cost of equity, the most likely scenario is that the company's book multiple remains close to where it is (0.57), resulting in limited share-price capital gains for qualifying shareholders.

Conclusion

To sum up, FONAR has all the hallmarks of being a reputable value play, but the fact of the matter is that the company is not making enough money, much less than equity investors are being asked to risk. Therefore, due to the concerning technical trends and muted growth rates, compounded by the fact that FONAR's ROE remains significantly behind the company's cost of equity, we are downgrading FONR to a 'Hold' for now. We look forward to continued coverage.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: An Economy Built On Fear: Will Rate Cuts Save It?
data.description:
Introduction

We got some massive news for stock market investors this week, with the JOLTS, Productivity and Costs, and Employment Situation reports coming out. It can be overwhelming following all of the data and trying to make sense of it, so today I will clear out everything that feels “off” right now. Of course, we will see what it means for the rate cuts this month, what to prepare for, and what you should do with your portfolio.

So, the news is that job openings are no longer outpacing people looking for work. It happened for the first time since the middle of 2021. It doesn’t sound like a big deal until you realize it changes the psychology of the labor market from abundance to scarcity, and in scarcity, businesses turn into a saving rather than a growing mode, slowing down the economy.

The reason is pretty simple: once that change happens, managers and business owners switch from looking forward, planning, and expending mindset, to preserving what they already have. Companies are firing slightly more, although not by much, and it looks like instead of hiring, they are simply holding the workers that are already employed, and trying to squeeze more output from them. It’s not surprising, really. The economy has been going through a constant uncertainty in the past six months with tariffs and other political decisions, and in the uncertain conditions, people tend to freeze and to wait it all out patiently.

What The Data Shows

Officially, there are 7.2 million openings for July, 5.3 million hires, and 5.3 million total separations. These numbers take us in two directions at the same time.

First, the job market isn’t falling apart (separations aren’t jumping), but it isn’t speeding up either, because hires are not out from their recent pace. Adding to this that quits are about 3.2 million, and layoffs are around 1.8 million (employers aren’t cutting in big waves), and the picture becomes pretty clear. It means that people are keeping the jobs they have, they are not looking for anything better, and employers are not making huge layoffs. It’s like everyone is on pause. Companies are allowing attrition to do the work, meaning once someone quits, they are not replacing those people, holding decisions until there’s more clarity.

Openings in health care, social assistance, and arts and entertainment were especially weak, and the overall hiring rate barely moved, which together means fewer new roles are being posted and the ones that do open aren’t being filled quickly. Business owners are waiting to see how revenue, costs, and most importantly, policy behaves before they commit to anything.

When this pattern repeats for a few quarters, it usually doesn’t end up in a surge in joblessness. It climbs up slowly and steadily, exactly like the 4.3% that we saw this month. We can also notice the trend by taking a look at different metrics, like companies are slower to replace people who leave, internal moves replacing external hires, promotions tied to team growth get delayed to better times, and existing staff are asked to do more work/give more hours. I am sure some of these effects you could notice by talking to your friends, or at your own work, and others are evident in the labor productivity report that came out yesterday.

Because output increased 4.4% while hours only increased 1.1% in Q2, nonfarm productivity increased 3.3% (SAAR), which is exactly what I mentioned before: more output from about the same amount of labor. In manufacturing, productivity was +2.5% with hours down 0.1%; durables +3.2% (hours up just 0.3%) and nondurables +1.9% (hours −0.6%). This indicates that companies are meeting demand by optimizing processes and software, not by adding shifts. Basically the same in a slightly different language - focus on getting more out of what you already have. Productivity is up 5.7%, output is up 7.0%, and hours are up 1.2%, even in the nonfinancial corporate sector. Efficiency gains are offsetting wage growth, which is good for margins but also indicates that "do more with less" is the operating mantra. At the same time, real hourly pay increased 2.6% while unit labor costs increased just 1.0%. People are finally getting slightly higher checks.

Basically, we just arrived at the explanation for this dramatic slowdown in the employment situation we see today. When output is up 4.4% and hours are up only 1.1%, and openings are below the number of job seekers, you don't see a hiring surge. Instead, you see a careful labor market, where companies keep people, replacing them slowly, only if necessary. The unemployment rises, but not dramatically, and everyone waits to see what happens next in the economy.

Employment Situation

There were only 22,000 new jobs added in August, far less than was projected before, and the unemployment rate increased slightly to 4.3%. Health care added 31,000 jobs, making it the main growing sector, but it was still slower than its average of 42,000 jobs per month over the past year. Social assistance added 16,000 jobs.

But there were sectors that dragged the overall results down. Around 15,000 jobs were lost in the federal government, mining/oil and gas lost 6,000 jobs, and wholesale trade lost 12,000 jobs. 12,000 jobs were lost in manufacturing in the last month, and overall 78,000 in the last year. Transportation equipment lost 15,000 jobs, partly because of a strike that will end, but the main point is that factories are not hiring because they are waiting for rates and policy to become more stable.

Average hourly wages increased by 0.3% month over month, or 3.7% year over year, to $36.53. Production/nonsupervisory earnings increased by 0.4% month over month to $31.46. The workweek remained at 34.2 hours for the third month, while manufacturing hours decreased to 40.0 with 2.9 hours of overtime (optimizing the machines and software).

Labor force participation is 62.3% and the employment-population ratio is 59.6%, both 0.4 percentage points lower than last year, meaning that fewer people are working or even looking for a job. The number of people who are not in the labor force but say they want one is at 6.4 million, which is about 722,000 more than a year ago. New entrants fell by 199,000 following last month's bump. Part-time for economic reasons is 4.7 million, with little change here, and revisions reduced June–July payrolls by 21,000. In short, it's a market where employers are cautious about hiring, there are fewer new positions available, wages are stable, and businesses are getting more out of their current workforce rather than growing, totally the same conclusion as before.

Will the rates be cut?

In my opinion, the answer is a 100% yes. In fact, every single one of this week's reports gives the Fed grounds to begin reducing rates, take them all together, and it doesn’t look like next week's reports will be able to change it. Besides, the Fed is pressured to the extent of cutting the rates, so when the pressure is there, and the reports point in the same direction, there are almost no reasons to refuse. For the first time since 2021, the number of job openings has fallen below the number of unemployed, indicating a cooling of hiring. The economy is on pause, as evidenced by the August payrolls, which were essentially flat at +22,000 with unemployment at 4.3%, wages steady at +0.3% m/m, +3.7% y/y, and the workweek stuck at 34.2 hours. Furthermore, in Q2, real hourly pay increased 2.6% and productivity increased 3.3% (SAAR) while unit labor costs increased only 1.0%. When combined, softer labor and decreasing cost pressure allow the Fed to change course without fear of boosting inflation (at least, not by the cuts alone).

Will cuts help?

They will help, but they cannot resolve all of the fundamental issues on their own. Lower rates decrease monthly payments on cars and mortgages and make it cheaper for businesses to borrow, so projects that were too expensive can now move forward, slowly boosting hiring and demand.

But the biggest thing holding businesses back right now is uncertainty, and while uncertainty around rate cuts is one of them, it’s not the only topic people are getting stressed over. The fact that tariffs go on, then off, up to 120%, down to 10% with no stop and no way of knowing where they will go next, is making people not want to plan. Regulations shift in healthcare, in housing, in education, and in other previously stable areas of people's lives (relatively stable at least), are not adding certainty to the mix. Companies that are being led by the same people who hate not knowing what will happen, don’t want to make big commitments when they can’t plan even a few months. So instead of opening new roles, opening businesses, expanding, they make current employees work more, and push small efficiency projects to squeeze more out of the same people.

That’s why we’re seeing fewer job openings, flat hiring, and higher productivity at the same time. Companies are just choosing to wait rather than grow. Rate cuts can help by lowering borrowing costs and improving sentiment. But if the rules of this game keep changing, many businesses will still hold off on hiring and expansion.

data.title: Sportsman's Warehouse Q2: Still A Hairy Situation, Too Risky For Me
data.description:
Sportsman's Warehouse Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:SPWH) Q2 '25 results were mixed. Sales are up, albeit with very easy comps last year, and margins failed to leverage meaningfully.

The company is taking too long to leverage results, and in its current condition, each quarter of losses reduces the company's potential cash runway.

I do not believe the name is attractive as an investment in this condition, and therefore maintain a Hold rating.

Q2 '25 results

Sales up on easy comps: SPWH posted sales up 2%, with same-store sales up 2% and ecommerce up 3%. For H1 '25, the results are the same on the top line.

This is a positive development considering that last year SPWH was shedding sales, but the company is far from recovering what it lost last year. Sales were down 13% in Q1 '24 and almost 10% in 2Q24.

That is, the quarter had easy comps, and yet only met LSD growth. This is still a very challenged company in terms of sales. We should remember that sales have been falling almost uninterruptedly since 2021.

Difficulty lifting margins: In the same vein, after a year of inventory clearing last year, the company managed to grow gross margins by 80bps in the quarter, and 50bps for the half year, but SG&A delevered 40bps.

The problem of the lack of leverage is that the company is still posting severe net losses, $30 million in H1 '25. In fact, as we will see below, it is still guiding for net losses for the year.

Debt and inventories up: The company added a lot of inventories, up $80 million in the year. This was planned, according to management, in order to avoid tariffs. The problem is that this exposes the company to the need to do promotions to clear inventories if demand is less than expected, and that adds debt. Indeed, debt is up almost $100 million so far in the year, although it should be down after Q3 (high selling hunting season).

The company commented that its cash focus will be on debt repayment, commenting that inventories should peak in Q2, and that it is not planning to open stores (CAPEX at 50% of D&A).

Valuation is not attractive given the risks

The company is guiding for sales up 3.5% YoY, and adjusted EBITDA of $33 to $45 million (a wide range). This is an improvement from last year, but not enough to cover expenses.

Interest will probably hover around $12/14 million for the year. CAPEX was guided at $20/25 million (D&A is actually twice as much, and includes lease expenses), and SBC will probably move around $3/4 million.

That means the company is barely able to cover expenses in the low range of the EBITDA guidance, and not cover them even on the upper range if D&A is considered instead of CAPEX.

If adjusted EBITDA's upper range is reached, the company may be able to post $10 million in cash-based profits (assuming no taxes, given net losses from D&A), which could be considered a sufficient return for a market cap of around $100 million.

The problem is that this leaves the potential for a more turbulent H2 '25 and FY26 from a macro perspective. Outdoor activities are a highly discretionary category; many articles are imported, and therefore, sales or margins could decrease significantly.

If things get more hairy, the company will probably survive, as it has access to as much as $100 million in credit liquidity, but its situation will worsen significantly. SPWH was able to maintain a positive CFO in FY23 and FY24 only by decreasing inventories, an alternative that seems less viable today.

That is, the company seems to be in a difficult line, and the return is barely acceptable only in the best-case scenario, without consideration of other possibilities.

For this reason, I believe the company is not fairly valued, and is not an opportunity, except for speculative positions under the conviction that it can turn around the business quickly.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: Still Trading For A Premium Despite Concerns
data.description:
2025 has been a mixed year for global hotelier Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc. (NYSE:HLT). The firm's financial performance is on track to be a shade weaker than management initially expected, with the company not totally immune to the weaker macro backdrop that has weighed on so many consumer cyclicals in recent months. That hasn't prevented the stock from delivering a respectable performance, though, with a double-digit year-to-date return not only better than close comps/peers, but also the broader consumer discretionary space.

Hilton's valuation was my only real concern when I last covered it in November. The firm's long-term growth prospects are bright, while its fee-based business model also produces very high-quality profits—and that means there's plenty of cash for capital returns. Still, a ~36x multiple on earnings per share seemed to bake all of that in, so I rated it a "Hold."

Given the shift in economic sentiment at that time, now looks like a good time to review the investment case here. I remain "Neutral," with Hilton still commanding a significant premium to peers and to its past valuation.

Softer Demand Set To Weigh In The Near Term

As many readers will know, Hilton is really a fee business rather than a hotel owner-operator. Of the 1.3 million rooms in its system, over 1 million operate under franchise agreements. Most of the rest operate under managed contracts. Only around 1% of its rooms are in hotels that are actually owned or leased by Hilton itself.

Under a typical franchise agreement, Hilton will receive a cut of room revenue, leaving owners fully exposed to hotel-level profitability. Managed contracts are slightly different. As the name implies, these see Hilton take on the management of the property, albeit the running costs are a pass-through line (the owners will reimburse Hilton for this). In addition to receiving a percentage cut of room revenue, these contracts typically come with a share of hotel-level earnings as well, making them slightly more cyclical than franchise agreements.

Given the above, Hilton's earnings are largely driven by the number of rooms in its system and the average revenue per available room ('RevPAR'). In turn, RevPAR is a function of hotel occupancy and the room rate.

While this results in a relatively defensive earnings stream, Hilton is not immune to wider shifts in the economy, and with roughly 64% of its rooms located in the United States, it has seen tariff-related uncertainty creep into its results. With that, U.S. RevPAR inched up just 0.2% year-on-year over the first six months of 2025, with growth slowing by around 120 basis points year-on-year. This was driven by a lower occupancy rate, which fell around 30 bps year-on-year to 71.9%. Following April's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, Q2 was markedly worse, with U.S. RevPAR falling by 1.5% year-on-year.

Hilton is seeing more resilient trends elsewhere, as Q2 RevPAR was still positive in its other geographic segments. However, the sheer weight of its U.S. footprint led to a 0.5% year-on-year decline in firm-wide RevPAR last quarter.

Given this softness, management has walked down full-year guidance. Whereas firm-wide RevPAR was initially seen rising by 2%-3% in 2025, this has been reduced to 0%-2%. Profit guidance has also taken a slight hit, with adjusted EBITDA now seen around 1% lower at the midpoint ($3.68 billion).

Longer-Term Tailwinds Remain In Place

While economic concerns have weighed on RevPAR, it is important to point out that Hilton's prospects still look bright. I am particularly bullish on system growth. Higher occupancy rates, direct booking capabilities, and large, growing loyalty programs are just three of the advantages that branded players enjoy over independent hotels. This can drive market share gains over time, resulting in above-average room growth. Indeed, as you can see below, there is a large gap between Hilton's current market share and its share of rooms in development.

Demand for Hilton's brands among prospective franchisees appears to be strong. Hilton ended last quarter with just over 1.3 million rooms, more than 7% higher than the year-ago period. While full-year RevPAR guidance has been lowered, guidance for 6%-7% net system growth remains unchanged. Interestingly, this rate of growth is even higher than back in 2018 and 2019 (chosen to normalize for COVID), when rooms grew by ~6% annually. This acceleration is arguably even more impressive than it looks at first, as interest rates are much higher today than they were back then (this being a headwind for hotel construction costs).

Supporting this, Hilton is seeing a high share of its new rooms come from conversions of existing hotels. Management mentioned on the Q2 call that these were responsible for around one-third of its openings last quarter, which is near the top end of its recent historical average.

Strong system growth is also helping to offset the softness in RevPAR. While guidance has been lowered a touch, earnings are still growing at a strong clip. Hilton posted just over $1 billion in adjusted EBITDA last quarter, up 10% year-on-year.

Various factors can help Hilton maintain a strong rate of earnings growth in the future. First, the company's pipeline continues to expand, clocking in at around 511K rooms at the end of last quarter, up around 4% year-on-year. Relative to its existing system, Hilton enjoys a peer-leading room pipeline, and this can sustain a solid rate of growth for many years to come.

Second, there is a lot of operating leverage in Hilton's business, as fee growth does not require much by way of extra costs to service. Last quarter, Hilton reported a 75.2% adjusted EBITDA margin, up 300 basis points year-on-year and around 1,400 basis points higher than in the equivalent quarter in pre-COVID 2019. Fundamentally, margin expansion means that profit growth can outpace growth in revenue. This is happening. While fees grew by 8% year-on-year last quarter, adjusted EBITDA growth was around 200 basis points above that.

Finally, because Hilton is largely a franchisor, it doesn't need to invest heavily to bring new rooms online, as construction costs are borne by hotel owners. To that end, the company's current pipeline has resulted in less than $500 million in committed contract acquisition costs—much lower than the $60 billion in third-party investment.

This also helps to explain why Hilton has spent just $155 million on capital expenditures and contract acquisition costs so far this year. CapEx consumes a small percentage of its EBITDA. In contrast, this means it can spend heavily on shareholder returns instead. Having already executed $1.9 billion through July, Hilton is planning around $3.3 billion in total capital returns this year, equal to ~5% of its current market cap. Given this is mainly conducted through buybacks, shares outstanding also fell by around 5% year-on-year through the first half of the year, contributing to around 14% growth in adjusted EPS.

The Valuation

Hilton stock trades for around $277 as I type, having gained around 9% since my last piece. As this is lower than its EPS growth, its valuation has actually de-rated a little, with the current quote mapping to just under 35x current-year consensus EPS, down from ~36x last time out.

Still, this remains a rich valuation. First, it appears to represent a healthy premium over its closest peers, with InterContinental Hotels Group PLC (IHG) at ~24.4x consensus 2025 EPS and Marriott International, Inc. (MAR) at ~26.4x.

Moreover, Hilton's valuation is also running ahead of its own historical marks. Because its earnings were heavily impacted by COVID, I like to use pre-2020 levels as a guide. We can see from the chart above that Hilton's market cap averaged around $25 billion between 2018 and 2019. Adjusted net income in those years was $1.04 billion (in 2018) and $1.13 billion (in 2019). That suggests Hilton was trading for around 22x earnings, which is roughly 35% below its current multiple.

Between system growth, RevPAR growth, margin expansion, and share buybacks, I think Hilton can average double-digit annualized EPS growth over the long run. Its quarterly dividend, currently $0.15 per share, adds a modest 0.2% on top. However, given its current elevated multiple, valuation de-rating remains a risk. Accordingly, I remain "Neutral."

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Buy Before The Market Realizes Its Folly
data.description:
Bristol-Myers Squibb: Market Still Not Fully Convinced

Same old, same old. What do I mean? While Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (NYSE:BMY) surpassed Wall Street's estimates back in late July for its Q2 earnings, the stock has continued to struggle for traction since then. Therefore, it seems like the day of redemption carries on for BMY investors who thought elation could have arrived earlier for them to declare the seemingly elusive victory.

Back in late June, I indicate that Bristol-Myers Squibb still faces persistent pressure on the losses from its legacy products. While the burgeoning partnership with BioNTech (BNTX) should bolster its long-term potential in oncology, the market is justified to feel concerned with the uncertainties linked to a gamut of challenges hampering the biopharma leader.

Nevertheless, BMY management was quick to highlight several catalysts could spur a valuation upgrade for investors who have the patience to wait out the current slog. These milestones were aptly underscored at its recent Q2 earnings update, as the company aims to provide a relief of sorts for investors who might be concerned with the loss of exclusivity headwinds that could affect BMY's earnings profile through 2028.

Still, Bristol-Myers Squibb accentuated the need for investors to take a longer-term outlook on its bullish narrative. While the above-mentioned milestones could impel dip buyers to accumulate BMY shares while they are arguably undervalued, execution risks still cannot be downplayed completely. And the revenue growth dynamics are clearly obfuscated by multitudinous challenges that could have catalyzed a recovery based on the strength of its growth portfolio, notwithstanding the LOE hurdles that BMY aims to surmount effectively.

BMY: Growth Portfolio Gaining Clout

As a reminder, BMY management didn't pull any punches as it sought to convince investors into the robustness of its growth portfolio, which saw a welcomed 18% YoY revenue increase in Q2. Thus, it has been instrumental in helping to allay fears of the anticipated challenges that have affected its legacy segment, which is expected to decline by a midpoint of 16% for the full year.

Nevertheless, management indicated that it will begin experimenting with a direct to consumer pilot for Eliquis later this month, augmenting its ability to offer discounted versions to consumers, while reportedly bypassing the profits that could have gone to the PBMs. Accordingly, DTC consumers should expect a discount of more than 50% below the list price, even though the 2028 LOE should still stymie a more confident outlook in the medium term.

Management has maintained its commitment to continue vigorously with its cost-cutting program, which is expected to reach $2B through 2027, coupled with its debt reduction program potentially reaching $10B by the first half of 2026. Moreover, while the topline growth prospects could face an extended period of tepid or even negative growth, the company still expects to generate solid profitability, helping to mitigate execution risks on its much-prized dividends. As a result, I think that should placate investors who are still awaiting more credence from its upcoming catalysts through 2027, before deciding whether to return with even more fervor into buying BMY's dips.

BMY: Robust Profitability Should Allay Fears

As a reminder, Bristol-Myers Squibb is still expected to generate sector-leading profitability, which should afford the confidence for income investors to stay the course. And based on BMY's forward dividend yield of 5.4%, it's also best in class when compared to the healthcare median of 1.4%, providing support for its already embattled valuation. And with seemingly low risks of seeing that dividend being eliminated or lowered notwithstanding BMY's LOE related risks, I believe it's credible to contemplate that income investors aren't likely to be perturbed with the company's ability to reignite its topline growth in the near term.

Still, I don't think we can simply ignore the possibility that the market has justifiably downgraded its optimism on BMY, as it assesses an inflection point after through FY2026. However, given the associated LOE risks through the next three years, I wouldn't lay blame on buyers who want to see more proof points on the execution of its pipeline and the progress in its milestones before returning to the fray with higher conviction on the stock.

Buy Or Sell BMY Stock?

Yet, shouldn't we also consider the plausibility that BMY appears to have bottomed out after having consolidated since its July lows? Its forward EBITDA multiple of 8x is significantly below the sector median of 15.5x. In my opinion, The market has arguably considered the challenges that could hobble the stock's recovery over the next three years. Also, my observation is corroborated by the fact that the de-rating through July didn't break down below the July 2024 levels.

In other words, I think unless the company decides to lower its guidance through next year, the risk/reward of picking up BMY's undervalued shares at the current levels seems increasingly attractive.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: abrdn Global Dynamic Dividend Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The abrdn Global Dynamic Dividend Fund underperformed its benchmark over the three-month period ending 31 July 2025 due to stock selection and, to a lesser extent, sector allocation.1

In terms of stocks that detracted, London Stock Exchange Group’s (OTCPK:LDNXF) shares declined as investors grappled with a modest slowdown in its reported annual subscription value metric as well as the risk of AI disruption to its Data & Analytics business segment.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) reduced its top and bottom line guidance for the second half of 2025 due to slower than expected sales of GLP-1 drugs. It also announced a new CEO, promoted from within, which disappointed investors who had hoped that the company would look externally for fresh leadership.

In addition to tariff-related uncertainty over pharmaceutical imports into the US, Sanofi (SNY) reported disappointing late-stage pipeline data for its chronic obstructive pulmonary disease drug candidate, along with mixed second-quarter results.

The top detractors from relative performance also included not owning NVIDIA (NVDA) and Meta Platforms (META), which pay only minimal dividends, and Amazon.com (AMZN), which does not pay a dividend.

In terms of individual stocks that positively contributed, Broadcom (AVGO) was among the Fund’s top contributors as investors became more bullish on the growth prospects for AI accelerators and, in particular, the company’s custom application-specific integrated circuits business.

Oracle (ORCL) reported strong fiscal fourth-quarter earnings in June, including upward revisions to expected revenue growth in the coming years, and later announced a major partnership with OpenAI for its Stargate project, which is expected to generate $30 billion in annual revenue beginning in fiscal year 2028.

Shares of Goldman Sachs (GS) rose on signs of improved capital markets activity and as investors anticipated the Trump administration easing the regulatory burden, including lower capital requirements.

Market review

Global equity markets ended higher over the period. In May, the US and China agreed at talks in Geneva to implement substantial reciprocal tariff reductions for 90 days: the US lowered additional tariffs on Chinese goods from 145% to 30% and China reduced retaliatory duties on US imports from 125% to 10% – measures which were more substantial and introduced earlier than investors had anticipated. The agreement was reaffirmed at the US–China trade talks in London in June. Nonetheless, uncertainty persisted over its eventual outcome, with further negotiations expected in the weeks ahead. Also in June, the US doubled tariffs on global steel and aluminium imports (except from the UK) to 50%, adding to trade policy uncertainty.

During the same month, investors’ focus shifted to rising tensions in the Middle East after Israel launched strikes against Iran over its nuclear programme. The situation escalated as Iran retaliated with missile attacks on Israel, prompting large-scale US airstrikes on Iranian nuclear facilities. A US-brokered ceasefire between Israel and Iran, agreed before the end of June, eased tensions somewhat.

In July, there were further tariff changes, including a high-profile US–EU agreement cutting US tariffs on EU goods to 15% from the previously threatened 30%, alongside other deals with key partners that lowered duties as well as measures that increased them on selected imports. Talks with Mexico and China were extended into August.

Despite continued disinflation, investors began to anticipate a more measured pace of monetary easing as most central banks maintained a data-dependent stance. Corporate first-quarter earnings generally exceeded expectations, particularly among companies exposed to artificial intelligence (AI), though concerns over China’s property sector and the Russia–Ukraine war persisted.

Outlook & strategy

Macroeconomic factors remain as unpredictable as ever. While trade and tariff negotiations now appear to be nearing resolution, the immediate fear of an inflationary spike due to the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements has begun to ease. Many countries have secured lower-than-initially-feared tariff rates, which should help temper further inflationary pressures. However, overall tariffs remain higher than pre-“Liberation Day” levels, leaving lingering concerns over their long-term economic impact. At the same time, global central banks remain divided in their policy stance, with some signalling caution due to persistent inflation, while others are beginning to cut rates amid signs of slowing growth. Labour markets are showing early signs of softening, and consumer sentiment is mixed, adding to the uncertainty surrounding the near-term direction of the economy.

Nevertheless, our focus remains at the stock level, ensuring the portfolio is well diversified across both regions and sectors, and robust enough to preserve capital during periods of market weakness.

Important Information

International investing entails special risk considerations, including currency fluctuations, lower liquidity, economic and political risks, and differences in accounting methods; these risks are generally heightened for emerging market investments.

Shares of closed-end funds may trade above (a premium) or below (a discount) the net asset value (NAV) of the Fund. The net asset value (NAV) is the value of an entity’s assets less the value of its liabilities. The market price is the current price at which an asset can be bought or sold. There is no assurance that the Fund will achieve its investment objective. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

data.title: August Jobs Data Is Worse Than July
data.description:
Introduction

The Federal Reserve's hesitancy around lowering interest rates primarily stems from being in an economic "rock and hard place" scenario. While inflation is running hotter-than-expected in the core category, and still above the 2% inflation target (which may now be in flux), the jobs market is getting weaker.

The last major jobs report in July was a doozy. It was so bad that President Trump fired the head of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the government division that produces the reports.

Many analysts were pessimistic going into the August report for a few reasons, namely the JOLTS report, a smaller survey than the jobs report, but one that tracks job openings, hirings, and separations.

The JOLTS Report Was Rough

The headline that came out of this report was that job openings fell to a low we haven't seen in a year. We're on a downtrend since May, with little precedent in the last year of going any lower.

At the same time, layoffs are rising from May, up to a relative high in July, the last high coinciding with the last openings low, September 2024.

What I think is important to note about the JOLTS data was how tight the job market is for workers. While I've been hearing this anecdotally about "job hugging," as a trend, we can visualize it in the ratio of quits to layoffs, showing us that in recent months, folks have been holding onto jobs more and more, while layoffs rise. These two figures are trending in opposite directions of each other — worse is that both are trending in the wrong direction, causing this ratio to accelerate from last year.

The August Jobs Report

Going into this report, I was expecting a poor result. More so, I was fearful that we would have further revisions to the last two months to the downside. We received mixed results on this front, losing jobs and going negative in June while adding jobs to July's figure.

The US added 22,000 jobs in August, according to current estimates.

Another red flag for the economy, the unemployment rate is still rising, now rising above the ceiling of the channel it had been in since it crossed over 4%. This could be just a blip crossover to 4.3%, but could also be the continuation of a trend; time will tell.

Taking a look at the report itself, we see a continuing and worsening of the two groups that I've been watching closely for a while, "part-time for economic reasons" which includes folks who want full-time work but can only find part-time work, and discouraged workers, who have given up looking for work. Both categories are up MoM, but mixed YoY.

These folks affect the non-headline unemployment figures, which still tell us a considerable amount about the economy. Present figures in these categories is not catastrophic, but the trend is worrying. These are categories that could rise considerably before they get media attention, so it's worth following specifically in the report.

While the headline figure is 4.3%, U-6, a broader measure that includes these underemployed and discouraged workers, is now sitting at 8.1%.

Healthcare Domination Continues

What's interesting to is to watch the continuing domination of the trend for healthcare to be the largest job add category, while other categories that are expected to rise have not, like goods manufacturing.

In July, if you excluded healthcare jobs, the total jobs figure was negative.

Here's a reminder of how deeply healthcare employment affects our economy, and how much of a "maintenance economy" the US has become.

So when looking at the jobs report for August, how'd we do?

The trend unfortunately continues: losing jobs in goods manufacturing and professional services (thanks, AI), while gaining in private education, healthcare, and social assistance.

Unsurprisingly, the shed of government jobs continues unabated. As expected, the losses are concentrated in Federal positions, not state or local.

What Will the Fed Do?

Ah — the million-dollar question. I don't believe the Fed has deviated from its trajectory that I called last month, where I believe we will see a 25bp rate cut at the next FOMC meeting later this month, and likely at least 1 more cut following that before the end of the year.

This isn't a controversial call, although I believe I am a bit less wavering about it than the financial media can be. The Fed needs to cut rates if they want the labor market to stabilize, and the falling jobs numbers — now with a recent month in the negatives — give them very good reason to do so.

Of course, you can't tackle inflation if rates are too low, so I don't expect the Fed to drop more than 50bp this year. They still have to keep rates high to fight off the inflation we have, which is up to 2.9% on core PCE.

We will get our rate cut, and I expect the market to love it, so long as it comes paired with dovish Fedspeak at the presser afterward.

Now, the futures market is pricing in what was once thought of as an impossibility over the last day, week, and month: a 50bp cut in September. The futures traders are giving it a 14% probability. A week ago, odds of no cut were at nearly-14%, but now sit at 0%.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the takeaway from the labor market is that the trends we were already seeing pop up over the summer are continuing to manifest. We see them manifesting in the August jobs report as a low monthly jobs figure, rising unemployment rate, and revisions of past months into negative territory.

JOLTS data, adjacent jobs data that helps us keep track of trends in the labor market, are also showing weakness, with the ratios of openings and hirings to layoffs and quits showing weakness for workers across the board.

All of this culminates into an unemployment rate that is higher than the last few years, but still relatively depressed. Keeping it depressed is a job of the Fed, who will likely cut their key interest rate figure later this month in response to the weakening labor market we have.

I expected a 25bp cut, but we may see more if the speculators are right. For now, I'm not among them. Hoping for a 50bp cut this month is a little too optimistic.

Thanks for reading.
